equivalent-settings-calculator.beginner: Different sensor sizes produce different depth of field and field of view even at the same focal length and aperture. Equivalent settings let you find the lens and aperture combination on a second camera that reproduces the same framing and background blur. This is essential when comparing gear across formats or switching between camera systems.

0.heading: The Crop Factor
0.text: Every sensor has a crop factor relative to the 35mm full-frame standard. APS-C sensors have a factor around 1.5×, Micro Four Thirds 2×, and medium format around 0.79×. Multiplying the actual focal length by the crop factor gives the full-frame equivalent field of view. A 35mm lens on APS-C frames like a 52.5mm on full frame.

1.heading: Equivalent Aperture & DoF
1.text: To match depth of field across formats, multiply the f-number by the same crop factor. An f/1.4 lens on Micro Four Thirds produces DoF equivalent to f/2.8 on full frame (1.4 × 2 = 2.8). This is why full-frame cameras are prized for shallow DoF — you can use physically faster lenses at wider actual apertures to achieve blur that smaller sensors cannot replicate.

2.heading: Equivalent ISO & Total Light
2.text: Matching exposure brightness across formats requires adjusting ISO by the crop factor squared. The larger sensor collects more total light for the same scene, which means it can use a lower ISO for equivalent noise performance. A full-frame sensor at ISO 800 produces similar noise to an APS-C sensor at ISO 356 and a Micro Four Thirds sensor at ISO 200.

0.label: Crop Factor
0.description: The ratio of the full-frame sensor diagonal to the target sensor diagonal. This single number drives all equivalence calculations for focal length, aperture, and ISO.

1.label: Sensor Size
1.description: Physically larger sensors capture more light and produce shallower DoF at equivalent framing. The equivalence calculator reveals exactly how much difference each format makes.

2.label: Available Lenses
2.description: Equivalence math may call for a lens that does not exist. A 17mm f/0.7 is the equivalent of 35mm f/1.4 on MFT — but no such lens is made. Real-world gear limits what is achievable.

0.text: When comparing camera systems, always compare at equivalent settings rather than matching f-numbers. An f/2.8 lens on Micro Four Thirds does not give the same background blur as f/2.8 on full frame — you need f/1.4 on MFT to match.

1.text: Use equivalence to plan lens purchases when switching systems. If you love the look of 85mm f/1.8 on full frame, look for a 56mm f/1.2 on APS-C or a 42.5mm f/0.95 on MFT for a similar rendering.

2.text: Remember that equivalence applies to depth of field and field of view, not to lens speed for exposure. An f/2.8 lens on any format still lets in the same amount of light per unit area — the larger sensor just collects more total light because of its greater area.

sourceSensor.definition: The sensor format of the camera whose settings you want to match. All equivalence calculations are relative to this starting point.

sourceFocalLength.definition: The actual focal length of the lens on your source camera. This will be converted to equivalent focal lengths on target formats.

sourceAperture.definition: The f-number on your source camera. The equivalent aperture on each target format determines matching depth of field.

targetSensor.definition: The sensor format you want to find equivalent settings for. The calculator applies the relative crop factor to compute matching focal length and aperture.

cropFactor.definition: The ratio between the diagonal of a 35mm full-frame sensor and the target sensor. Multiply focal length by crop factor for equivalent FOV; multiply aperture by crop factor for equivalent DoF.

equivalentFL.definition: The focal length on the target format that produces the same field of view as the source setup.

equivalentAperture.definition: The f-number on the target format that produces the same depth of field as the source setup. May be faster than any real lens.

0.scenario: You shoot portraits with an 85mm f/1.8 lens on a full-frame camera. A friend uses Micro Four Thirds (2× crop). What focal length does your friend need to match your field of view?
0.hint: Divide the full-frame focal length by the MFT crop factor to find the actual lens needed.
0.successMessage: Correct! 85mm / 2 = 42.5mm. Your friend needs a 42.5mm lens on Micro Four Thirds to get the same framing as your 85mm on full frame.
0.failureMessage: To match the field of view, divide by the crop factor: 85mm / 2 = 42.5mm. That is the actual focal length needed on the MFT body.

1.scenario: Your friend found a 42.5mm f/1.8 lens for their MFT camera. Will the background blur match your 85mm f/1.8 on full frame?
1.hint: Apply the crop factor to the aperture as well to find the equivalent DoF.
1.successMessage: Right! The 42.5mm f/1.8 on MFT gives equivalent DoF to 85mm f/3.6 on full frame (1.8 × 2 = 3.6). Your f/1.8 full-frame shot produces significantly more blur.
1.failureMessage: No — the MFT f/1.8 gives DoF equivalent to f/3.6 on full frame (1.8 × 2× crop = 3.6). To match your full-frame f/1.8 blur, your friend would need f/0.9 on MFT, which does not exist.

0.label: Yes — same f/1.8 means same blur

1.label: No — MFT f/1.8 equals full-frame f/3.6 depth of field

2.label: No — MFT produces more blur because the sensor is smaller

2.scenario: A photographer switching from full frame to APS-C (1.5× crop) wants to match the look of a 50mm f/1.4 on full frame. What equivalent lens and aperture do they need on APS-C?
2.hint: Divide focal length by the crop factor for equivalent FOV. Divide aperture by the crop factor for equivalent DoF.
2.successMessage: Exactly! 50 / 1.5 = 33mm for field of view, and f/1.4 / 1.5 = f/0.93 for matching DoF. Since no f/0.93 lens exists, the APS-C camera cannot fully replicate the full-frame look.
2.failureMessage: The correct answer is ~33mm f/0.93. Since 50 / 1.5 = 33.3mm and 1.4 / 1.5 = 0.93. No f/0.93 lens exists, so the full-frame look cannot be fully matched on APS-C.

1.label: 33mm f/0.93 — exact match, but no such lens exists
